text.title: Go Standard Project Layout
text.summary: Directory structure rules for Go projects: cmd/, internal/, pkg/, and layer sub-packages.

rule.statement: Place each binary entry point under cmd/<binary-name>/main.go.
rule.rationale: Multiple binaries in one module share internal packages without duplication. The directory name becomes the binary name.

rule.statement: Put all application code under internal/ to prevent external modules from importing it.
rule.rationale: The Go toolchain enforces internal/ visibility at compile time — no documentation or convention can be ignored accidentally.

rule.statement: Use pkg/ only for packages intended to be imported by external modules.
rule.rationale: pkg/ signals a public API contract. Putting application-specific logic here creates unintended coupling.

rule.statement: Organize internal/ into handler/, service/, repository/, model/, middleware/, config/ sub-packages.
rule.rationale: Each layer has one responsibility. Dependency flows handler → service → repository; no layer imports one above it.

rule.statement: handler/ translates HTTP requests to service calls and HTTP responses; no business logic.
rule.rationale: Mixing HTTP concerns with business logic makes unit testing require a full HTTP stack.

rule.statement: service/ contains all business logic; accepts and returns domain types; no HTTP or DB primitives.
rule.rationale: Services must be testable without a network or database — only domain types and interfaces allowed.

rule.statement: repository/ owns all database access; returns model types; no business logic.
rule.rationale: Isolating DB access enables swapping storage backends and testing with in-memory fakes.

rule.statement: Define interfaces in the consumer package, not the provider package.
rule.rationale: Consumer-side interfaces enforce the minimal surface needed and prevent import cycles.

section.title: Reference Layout
section.p: Canonical directory tree for a single-binary service:
section.reference: minimal handler with constructor and interface injection
